title: Erythroxylum novogranatense
taxonomy: Erythroxylum novogranatense is classified in the family Erythroxylaceae. This plant belongs to the order Malpighiales within the class Magnoliopsida. It is further classified as a member of the subclass Rosidae and the clade Asterids. The genus Erythroxylum is commonly known as the coca family, and it is native to South America. Erythroxylum novogranatense is closely related to Erythroxylum coca, another species of the genus.
uses: Erythroxylum novogranatense, also known as the wild coca plant, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. It contains compounds like alkaloids and phenolic acids, which are believed to have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. The plant's extracts have been used to treat conditions such as fever, respiratory issues, and digestive problems. Additionally, it may also help to reduce stress and improve cognitive function. However, its use as a medicinal plant is not well-studied, and more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness and safety.

intro: Erythroxylum novogranatense, also known as the wild coca plant, is a species of shrub native to the Andean region of South America. The leaves of this plant have been used for centuries in traditional medicine, offering various health benefits, including pain relief, improved cognitive function, and enhanced mental clarity, due to its ability to increase oxygenation of the brain. The bioactive constituents of E. novogranatense include alkaloids, such as cocaine and tetrahydrocannabinol (THC)-like compounds, as well as flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids. In herbal medicine, E. novogranatense is commonly prepared as a dried leaf tea or infused in a tincture, with some traditional applications also involving the use of the plant's roots and bark.
